RHDP LiteMaaS — Full Documentation (Plain Text for AI Ingestion)
================================================================
Source: https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/
GitHub: https://github.com/rhpds/rhpds.litemaas
Last updated: 2026-03-25

================================================================
WHAT IS RHDP LiteMaaS?
================================================================

LiteMaaS (Model as a Service) is the AI model access platform for the Red Hat Demo Platform (RHDP).
It provides workshop attendees, partners, and Red Hat engineers with a unified, governed interface to
production-grade AI models hosted on OpenShift AI — without requiring each user to manage credentials,
endpoints, or rate limits themselves.

LiteMaaS wraps the open-source LiteLLM proxy with a custom subscription and key management layer
(the LiteMaaS backend and frontend), adding per-user API key lifecycle management, subscription
approval workflows, usage analytics, and branding — all deployed natively on OpenShift.

Production deployment: The RHDP production instance runs at:
  - litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io (AI Proxy — OpenAI-compatible)
  - litellm-prod-frontend.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io (User Portal)
  - litellm-prod-admin.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/api (Admin API)

Namespace: litellm-rhpds
Cluster: maas.redhatworkshops.io

================================================================
KEY FEATURES
================================================================

Virtual API Key Management:
  Each user receives a personal virtual API key scoped to their subscription.
  Keys can have per-key TPM, RPM, and budget ceilings independently of other users.

Model Subscriptions:
  Users subscribe to models they need. Admins can mark models as "restricted" requiring
  approval before access is granted. Full audit trail on all state changes.

Usage Tracking & Analytics:
  Day-by-day incremental usage caching with multi-dimensional filtering (user, model,
  provider, API key). Export to CSV/JSON. Admin-only system-wide view.

Per-Key Rate & Budget Limits:
  Per-user configurable TPM (tokens per minute), RPM (requests per minute), max budget,
  budget duration, and soft budget thresholds enforced at the LiteLLM proxy layer.

Chat Playground:
  Built-in browser-based chat UI. Users can interactively test models they are subscribed
  to without writing code.

OpenShift OAuth Login:
  Three-tier RBAC (admin / adminReadonly / user) backed by OpenShift OAuth.

HA Architecture:
  All components run with multiple replicas. LiteLLM runs 3 replicas behind Redis for
  session and key caching. PostgreSQL 16 on a dedicated PVC.

Automated Key Cleanup:
  Daily cron job on the bastion host purges expired or stale keys (older than 30 days)
  from LiteLLM and syncs revocation status back to the LiteMaaS database.

================================================================
THREE-LAYER ARCHITECTURE
================================================================

Layer 1 — User Portal (LiteMaaS Frontend + Backend):
  React + PatternFly 6 frontend, Fastify backend API.
  Handles user authentication (OpenShift OAuth), subscription management, API key lifecycle,
  usage analytics, admin workflows, and branding.
  Runs as litellm-frontend and litellm-backend deployments (3 replicas each).
  Does not handle LLM inference — all model calls go through Layer 2.

Layer 2 — LiteLLM Proxy:
  Image: quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/litellm-non-root:main-v1.81.0-stable-custom
  Running as 3 HA replicas.
  Handles all actual model routing, virtual key enforcement (TPM/RPM/budget), request
  rate limiting, caching via Redis, and spend tracking.
  Stores its state in PostgreSQL. Exposes the OpenAI-compatible API.

Layer 3 — Model Serving (OpenShift AI / KServe):
  Red Hat OpenShift AI (RHOAI) running KServe predictors in the llm-hosting namespace.
  Models deployed as InferenceServices on GPU or CPU nodes.
  LiteLLM calls these internal ClusterIP services directly over the cluster network.

================================================================
USER ROLE HIERARCHY
================================================================

admin:
  - Assigned manually via promote-admin.sh or psql UPDATE
  - Full platform control: manage users, models, budgets, subscriptions, approve restricted
    access, view audit logs, configure branding, backup/restore

adminReadonly:
  - Manually assigned
  - Read-only admin views: view all users, analytics, audit logs, system state

user:
  - Automatically assigned on first OAuth login
  - Browse and subscribe to models, manage own API keys, view own usage, use Chat Playground

Important: Users must log in via OAuth at least once before being promoted to admin.
Direct database inserts break OAuth login because the OpenShift OAuth ID (UUID) is not set.

================================================================
AVAILABLE MODELS (as of 2026-03)
================================================================

--- On-Cluster GPU-Hosted (Self-Hosted) ---

granite-3-2-8b-instruct
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Context: 128K tokens
  Description: IBM Granite 3.2 8B, general-purpose instruction-tuned. Strong reasoning and coding.
  KServe predictor: granite-3-2-8b-instruct-predictor
  Replicas: 1

granite-4-0-h-tiny
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: IBM Granite 4.0 H Tiny. Low-latency. Best for simple Q&A and classification. Tool calling support.
  KServe predictor: granite-4-0-h-tiny-predictor
  Replicas: 1

llama-scout-17b
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Context: 400K tokens
  Description: Meta Llama 4 Scout 17B. Ideal for long-document analysis and large-context RAG.
  KServe predictor: llama-scout-17b-predictor
  Replicas: 2 (HA)

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b
  Capability: Chat, Reasoning
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: DeepSeek R1 Distill Qwen 14B. Chain-of-thought reasoning model.
  KServe predictor: deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b-predictor
  Replicas: 1

gpt-oss-120b
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: GPT OSS 120B. Large open-source model for complex generation and agentic workflows.
    NOTE: Also available as a Vertex AI pay-per-token model — same model ID, different backend.
  KServe predictor: gpt-oss-120b-predictor
  Replicas: 1

microsoft-phi-4
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: Microsoft Phi-4. Compact model with strong reasoning relative to size.
  KServe predictor: microsoft-phi-4-predictor
  Replicas: 1

qwen3-14b
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: Alibaba Qwen 3 14B. Multilingual model with strong instruction following.
  KServe predictor: qwen3-14b-predictor
  Replicas: 2 (LOAD BALANCED: one on maas00, one on smc00)

codellama-7b-instruct
  Capability: Chat
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Context: 16K tokens
  Description: Meta CodeLlama 7B. Code generation, completion, debugging in Python, Java, Bash, etc.
  KServe predictor: codellama-7b-instruct-predictor
  Replicas: 1

Llama-Guard-3-1B
  Capability: Safety / Guardrails
  Runtime: vLLM on KServe
  Description: Meta Llama Guard 3 1B. Classifies content against 13 hazard categories.
    Returns "safe" or "unsafe" with category labels.
  KServe predictor: llama-guard-3-1b-predictor
  Replicas: 1

nomic-embed-text-v1-5
  Capability: Embeddings
  Runtime: OpenVino on KServe
  Dimensions: 768
  Context: 8192 tokens
  Description: Nomic Embed Text. Supports Matryoshka representation learning.
    Strong retrieval benchmarks. Ideal for semantic search and RAG.
  KServe predictor: nomic-embed-text-v1-5-predictor
  Replicas: 1

granite-docling-258m
  Capability: Document Conversion
  Runtime: KServe (CPU), Docling Serve v1.14.3
  Description: IBM Granite Docling 258M. Converts PDFs and Word documents to clean Markdown.
    Handles layout analysis, table extraction, figure detection, OCR.
  Endpoint: /docling/v1/convert/source
  KServe predictor: granite-docling-258m-predictor
  Replicas: 1

--- Google Vertex AI (Pay-Per-Token, Programmatic Only) ---

minimax-m2
  Capability: Chat, Agentic
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI Model Garden
  Context: 197K tokens
  Pricing: $0.30 input / $1.20 output per 1M tokens
  Description: MiniMax M2. Strong at multi-step tool use, coding, and office workflows.

qwen3-235b
  Capability: Chat, Reasoning
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI Model Garden
  Context: 131K tokens
  Pricing: $0.22 input / $0.88 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Qwen3 235B (MoE). Multilingual, complex reasoning, large context tasks.

gpt-oss-120b
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI Model Garden
  Context: 131K tokens
  Pricing: $0.09 input / $0.36 output per 1M tokens
  Description: GPT OSS 120B via Vertex AI. NOTE: Same model ID as on-cluster self-hosted version.

gpt-oss-20b
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI Model Garden
  Context: 131K tokens
  Pricing: $0.07 input / $0.25 output per 1M tokens
  Description: GPT OSS 20B. Cost-effective general tasks, lightweight agentic use.

claude-sonnet-4-6
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI (Anthropic via Vertex)
  Pricing: $3.00 input / $15.00 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6. Strong general-purpose reasoning, coding, and analysis.

claude-opus-4-6
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI (Anthropic via Vertex)
  Pricing: $5.00 input / $25.00 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6. Most capable Claude model for complex multi-step tasks.

claude-sonnet-4-5
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI (Anthropic via Vertex)
  Pricing: $3.00 input / $15.00 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.5. Previous-generation Sonnet with strong coding and reasoning.

claude-3-5-haiku
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI (Anthropic via Vertex)
  Pricing: $1.00 input / $5.00 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Anthropic Claude 3.5 Haiku. Fastest and most cost-efficient Claude model.
    Ideal for classification, summarization, and high-throughput light Q&A.

gemini-2.5-pro
  Capability: Chat
  Hosted on: Google Vertex AI (native Google model)
  Context: 1M tokens
  Pricing: $1.25 input / $10.00 output per 1M tokens
  Description: Google Gemini 2.5 Pro. Native Google model with 1M context window.
    Ideal for long-document analysis, multimodal workflows, and large-context tasks.

================================================================
API ENDPOINTS
================================================================

Base URL: https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io

Replace YOUR_API_KEY with your virtual key from the LiteMaaS portal.

--- Chat Completions ---
POST /v1/chat/completions

curl -X POST https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "granite-3-2-8b-instruct",
    "messages": [
      {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful AI assistant."},
      {"role": "user", "content": "Explain Kubernetes in three sentences."}
    ],
    "max_tokens": 256,
    "temperature": 0.7
  }'

--- Streaming ---
Add "stream": true to the request body.

--- Embeddings ---
POST /v1/embeddings

curl -X POST https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/v1/embeddings \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "nomic-embed-text-v1-5",
    "input": "Red Hat OpenShift is an enterprise Kubernetes platform."
  }'

--- Document Conversion (Docling) ---
POST /docling/v1/convert/source

curl -X POST https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/docling/v1/convert/source \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "http_sources": [{"url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.09869"}],
    "options": {"output_format": "markdown"}
  }'

--- File Upload for Conversion ---
POST /docling/v1/convert/file

curl -X POST https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/docling/v1/convert/file \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -F "file=@/path/to/document.pdf" \
  -F 'options={"output_format": "markdown"}'

--- Tokenize ---
POST /v1/tokenize

curl -X POST https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/v1/tokenize \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "granite-3-2-8b-instruct",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "How many tokens is this?"}]
  }'

--- List Available Models ---
curl https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/v1/models \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"

================================================================
PYTHON SDK EXAMPLE
================================================================

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    api_key="YOUR_API_KEY",
    base_url="https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/v1"
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="granite-3-2-8b-instruct",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is Red Hat OpenShift?"}
    ]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

================================================================
DEPLOYMENT (Ansible Collection)
================================================================

Collection: rhpds.litemaas v0.3.2
GitHub: https://github.com/rhpds/rhpds.litemaas

Install:
  git clone https://github.com/rhpds/rhpds.litemaas.git
  ansible-galaxy collection install . --force

Deployment command:
  ansible-playbook playbooks/deploy_litemaas_ha.yml \
    -e ocp4_workload_litemaas_namespace=litemaas \
    -e ocp4_workload_litemaas_oauth_enabled=true \
    -e ocp4_workload_litemaas_ha_litellm_replicas=3

Deployment order:
  Namespace -> PostgreSQL 16 (StatefulSet + PVC) -> Redis 7 -> LiteLLM Proxy (3 replicas)
  -> LiteMaaS Backend (3 replicas) -> LiteMaaS Frontend (3 replicas) -> Routes + OAuthClient

Key Variables:
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_namespace — target OpenShift namespace (default: litemaas)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_oauth_enabled — enable OpenShift OAuth login (default: false)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_ha_litellm_replicas — LiteLLM replica count (default: 3)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_default_user_max_budget — default user budget in USD (default: 2000)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_default_user_budget_duration — budget reset period: daily/weekly/monthly (default: daily)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_postgres_storage_size — PVC size (default: 10Gi)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_ha_redis_image — Redis image (default: registry.redhat.io/rhel9/redis-7:latest)
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_litellm_image — LiteLLM proxy image
  ocp4_workload_litemaas_litellm_tag — LiteLLM image tag

Resource Summary (HA Deployment):
  LiteLLM proxy: 3 replicas, ~1500m CPU, ~1.5Gi memory
  LiteMaaS backend: 3 replicas, ~300m CPU, ~768Mi memory
  LiteMaaS frontend: 3 replicas, ~150m CPU, ~384Mi memory
  PostgreSQL 16: 1 StatefulSet, 500m CPU, 512Mi memory, 10Gi RWO storage
  Redis 7: 1 pod, 200m CPU, 256Mi memory
  Total: ~2650m CPU, ~3.4Gi memory, 10Gi storage

================================================================
DAY-2 OPERATIONS
================================================================

--- Upgrading Images ---
NS=litellm-rhpds
NEW_VERSION=0.5.0

# Update backend
oc set image deployment/litellm-backend \
  backend=quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/litemaas-backend:${NEW_VERSION} \
  -n ${NS}

# Update frontend (IMPORTANT: update both main container AND init container)
oc set image deployment/litellm-frontend \
  frontend=quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/litemaas-frontend:${NEW_VERSION} \
  init-frontend=quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/litemaas-frontend:${NEW_VERSION} \
  -n ${NS}

# Monitor rollout
oc rollout status deployment/litellm-backend -n ${NS}
oc rollout status deployment/litellm-frontend -n ${NS}

--- Scaling ---
# Scale LiteLLM to 5 replicas
oc scale deployment/litellm --replicas=5 -n litellm-rhpds

# Redis MUST be running for multi-replica LiteLLM
oc get pods -n litellm-rhpds -l app=litellm-redis

--- Adding Models ---
# After adding a model in LiteLLM admin UI, sync to LiteMaaS backend DB:
LITELLM_URL=$(oc get route litellm-prod -n litellm-rhpds -o jsonpath='https://{.spec.host}')
LITELLM_KEY=$(oc get secret litellm-secret -n litellm-rhpds \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.LITELLM_MASTER_KEY}' | base64 -d)

ansible-playbook playbooks/manage_models.yml \
  -e litellm_url="${LITELLM_URL}" \
  -e litellm_master_key="${LITELLM_KEY}" \
  -e ocp4_workload_litemaas_models_namespace=litellm-rhpds \
  -e ocp4_workload_litemaas_models_sync_from_litellm=true \
  -e '{"ocp4_workload_litemaas_models_list": []}'

--- Key Cleanup Cronjob ---
# Setup (run from rhpds.litemaas repo root)
./setup-key-cleanup-cronjob.sh litellm-rhpds

# Manual run
sudo /usr/local/bin/cleanup-litemaas-keys-litellm-rhpds.sh

# View logs
sudo tail -100 /var/log/litemaas-key-cleanup.log

================================================================
MONITORING
================================================================

Grafana: https://grafana-route-llm-hosting.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io
  Uses reencrypt TLS. Admin credentials in grafana-admin secret in llm-hosting namespace.

Get Grafana credentials:
  oc get secret -n llm-hosting | grep grafana-admin
  oc get secret <grafana-admin-secret> -n llm-hosting \
    -o jsonpath='{.data.GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD}' | base64 -d

ServiceMonitors in llm-hosting namespace (one per model predictor):
  granite-3-2-8b-instruct-metrics — vLLM metrics
  llama-scout-17b-metrics — vLLM metrics
  granite-4-0-h-tiny-metrics — vLLM metrics
  codellama-7b-instruct-metrics — vLLM metrics
  llama-guard-3-1b-metrics — vLLM metrics
  nomic-embed-text-v1-5-metrics — OpenVino metrics
  granite-docling-258m-metrics — vLLM metrics
  vllm-models — catch-all vLLM ServiceMonitor

Key Metrics:
  vllm:request_success_total — Request throughput
  vllm:time_to_first_token_seconds_bucket — TTFT latency (P50/P95/P99)
  vllm:num_requests_waiting — Queue depth
  vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc — KV cache usage
  DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL — GPU utilization %
  DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED — GPU framebuffer memory used

Healthy thresholds before a workshop:
  TTFT P95: <15s for ≤30 users, <25s for ≤60 users, <30s for ≤120 users
  Queue depth: <5 waiting requests
  GPU memory: <90% framebuffer
  KV cache utilization: <80%

Benchmark Tool:
  Role: ocp4_workload_litemaas_benchmark
  Simulates N concurrent users asking K questions in sequence.
  Pass/Fail criteria calculated from attendee count automatically.

LiteMaaS Built-in Analytics:
  Portal: litellm-prod-frontend.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io -> Admin -> Analytics
  Views: system overview, per-user, per-model, per-provider, per-API-key, export (CSV/JSON)

================================================================
COMMON TROUBLESHOOTING
================================================================

Issue 1: Models added in LiteLLM not showing in LiteMaaS
  Root cause: LiteMaaS has its own models table. LiteLLM admin UI changes don't auto-sync.
  Fix: Run manage_models.yml playbook with ocp4_workload_litemaas_models_sync_from_litellm=true
  Or: Enable LITELLM_AUTO_SYNC=true on litellm-backend deployment

Issue 2: Subscription fails with foreign key constraint violation
  Root cause: subscriptions.model_id references models.id but model not in LiteMaaS DB.
  Fix: Run sync playbook or INSERT INTO models directly as emergency fix.

Issue 3: Frontend showing old version after upgrade
  Root cause: Init container image not updated alongside main container.
  Fix: Update BOTH containers to same tag using oc set image for both frontend and init-frontend.

Issue 4: LiteLLM DB migration fails (Prisma errors, CrashLoopBackOff)
  Root cause: Schema migration didn't complete during upgrade.
  Fix: Scale to 1 replica, check logs, exec into pod to run Prisma migration manually.

Issue 5: Key sync broken between LiteMaaS and LiteLLM
  Root cause: Key created in LiteLLM not tracked in LiteMaaS api_keys table.
  Fix: Run sync playbook or trigger POST /api/admin/sync-keys endpoint.

Issue 6: Redis cache issues — stale model data
  Fix: oc rollout restart deployment/litellm -n litellm-rhpds
       oc exec -n litellm-rhpds deployment/litellm -- redis-cli FLUSHALL

Issue 7: OAuth callback fails
  Root cause: OAuthClient redirect URI mismatch or token expiry.
  Fix: Check OAuthClient redirectURIs match current frontend route hostname.

Issue 8: Model requests timing out
  Root cause: Large context requests taking longer than route timeout.
  Fix: Routes have 600s HAProxy timeout already set. For persistent issues, scale model replicas.

Issue 9: LiteLLM returning 401 / invalid API key
  Root cause: Key was deleted or Redis cache stale.
  Fix: oc rollout restart deployment/litellm -n litellm-rhpds
       Verify key exists: curl /key/info -H "Authorization: Bearer $LITELLM_MASTER_KEY"

Issue 10: Pods getting OOMKilled
  Root cause: Memory limits too low for workload.
  Fix: oc set resources deployment/litellm --limits=memory=4Gi -n litellm-rhpds

================================================================
GitOps MODEL MANAGEMENT
================================================================

Model servers on OpenShift AI are managed via GitOps (ArgoCD). Committing a new
InferenceService manifest to the repo automatically deploys it to the llm-hosting namespace.
This is separate from LiteMaaS deployment.

After a new model is deployed via GitOps, register it in LiteMaaS using either:
  - LiteLLM admin UI (Models -> Add Model) then run sync playbook
  - ocp4_workload_litemaas_models Ansible role
  - ocp4_workload_litemaas_litellm_models variable in deploy playbook

================================================================
KEY CREATION FOR WORKSHOPS
================================================================

Collection: rhpds.litellm_virtual_keys v0.2.0

The ocp4_workload_litellm_virtual_keys role creates per-user API keys automatically
for every workshop participant. Key configuration is defined in AgnosticV common.yaml:

litellm_virtual_keys_api_url: "https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io"
litellm_virtual_keys_admin_api_url: "https://litellm-prod-admin.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/api"
litellm_virtual_keys_models:
  - granite-3-2-8b-instruct
litellm_virtual_keys_max_budget: 5.0
litellm_virtual_keys_budget_duration: "30d"
litellm_virtual_keys_tpm: 150000
litellm_virtual_keys_rpm: 60

The role validates models exist, creates a scoped sk-... key per user, sets budget
and TPM/RPM limits, and delivers the key in lab credentials.

================================================================
BACKUP OPERATIONS
================================================================

Monthly automated pg_dump to S3 with VolumeSnapshot fallback.
Retention: 12 months for SQL dumps.

Setup backup cronjob:
  ./setup-litemaas-backup-cronjob.sh litellm-rhpds <s3-bucket>

Manual backup:
  oc exec -n litellm-rhpds litellm-postgres-0 -- \
    pg_dump -U litellm litellm | gzip > litellm-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql.gz

Restore:
  oc exec -i -n litellm-rhpds litellm-postgres-0 -- \
    psql -U litellm litellm < backup.sql
